overall_summary: Entergy Services argues that its existing state-regulated processes for interconnecting large loads in the Gulf South are working well and that a one-size-fits-all federal rule risks overstepping state jurisdiction and disrupting effective regional frameworks. The filing urges the Commission to limit its rule to transmission-level interconnections, raise the large-load threshold to at least 100 MW, respect regional differences including in MISO South, and address hybrid-load reliability concerns before acting.

quotes.text: We are concerned that a nationwide one-size fits all rule issued by the Commission could overstep State jurisdiction over retail service and disrupt effective State-level resource planning processes.
quotes.concern: Federal rule risks preempting state jurisdiction over retail service and resource planning

quotes.text: The Entergy Operating Companies believe our existing processes for the interconnection and service to large loads are working, as they balance new customer timelines with secure, reliable, and affordable service to all customers using existing regulatory frameworks.
quotes.concern: Existing state and utility processes are adequate and need not be displaced by federal rules

quotes.text: we agree that the study of load and generation additions together can result in a more efficient and cost-effective identification of transmission upgrades.
quotes.concern: Supports joint study of load and generation as a cost-efficient approach

quotes.text: we also agree that it is reasonable to take steps to deter speculative loads from delaying the interconnection of genuine projects.
quotes.concern: Supports measures to screen out speculative load interconnection requests

quotes.text: The Commission should be explicit about States retaining authority over retail service, consistent with the following principles.
quotes.concern: Commission must expressly preserve state authority over retail rates and service terms

quotes.text: any proposed rule should be explicit that the Commission is not ordering unbundling of retail services in States that have not implemented such unbundling, nor is it limiting States' authority to set the rates, terms and conditions of retail service to large loads.
quotes.concern: Rule must not compel retail unbundling or limit state authority over retail service agreements

quotes.text: it is important that the Commission avoid blanket rules that fail to account for such differences.  In particular, Entergy Services urges the Commission to avoid ordering any blanket rules that could interfere with retail jurisdictional resource planning that currently takes place in MISO South, as these processes are working well.
quotes.concern: Blanket federal rules would disrupt effective resource planning in MISO South

quotes.text: the Commission should allow individual utilities to submit proposals that specifically address the technical details involved in studying new loads or the requirement of operational limitations.
quotes.concern: Utilities should be able to submit region-specific study proposals rather than follow a uniform federal process

quotes.text: the Commission should eschew any formal or uniform federal queue requirement for loads.  A one-size-fits-all, Commission-regulated queue process has the potential to be inefficient, and fraught with litigation and conflict.
quotes.concern: Opposes a mandatory federal interconnection queue for loads

quotes.text: Entergy Services urges the Commission to consider alternative thresholds above 20 MW and recommend a threshold of at least 100 MW.  Anything less than a 100 MW threshold invites inefficiency, delay, and the potential for speculative projects to attempt to take advantage of any expedited process provided for under a final rule.
quotes.concern: 20 MW threshold is too low; recommends at least 100 MW to limit administrative burden and speculation

quotes.text: reducing the MW threshold for what is considered a 'large load' in each service territory from 100 MW to 20 MW, would increase the number of existing customers that would qualify as a large load under the proposed ANOPR by more than four times.
quotes.concern: 20 MW threshold would quadruple the qualifying load count in Entergy territories, creating administrative overload

quotes.text: the Commission must not impose any rules that could result in large loads paying less than their fair share of transmission costs, which includes the costs that they cause as a result of the need for upgrades or other appropriate transmission system modifications so that these new loads can be safely and securely interconnected.
quotes.concern: New large loads must bear the full cost of transmission upgrades they require

quotes.text: It would be overly simplistic to assume that a hybrid load's net injection or withdrawal impacts on the grid will be constant over any period of time.  Reliability issues can arise if a study of a hybrid facility assumes a certain level of energy injections onto the grid, but injections in fact exceed that level, or if no withdrawal rights are assumed but withdrawals occur.
quotes.concern: Studying hybrid facilities on a net-basis understates real reliability risks from variable injections and withdrawals

quotes.text: netting should not affect the way an interconnection request is studied.  Studying hybrid facilities in the manner contemplated by the proposed ANOPR may fail to identify needed network upgrades, because it will be blind to potentially substantial new loads, as long as those new loads are 'hybrid.'
quotes.concern: Net-basis hybrid study methodology can hide large withdrawal impacts and cause missed upgrade identification

quotes.text: Large loads should not be permitted to interconnect without consideration of available generation capacity.  For example, if existing generation is being used to serve a large load (rather than the construction of new generation), studies to ensure reliability and local resource adequacy will be needed.
quotes.concern: Resource adequacy and generation availability must be verified before a large load is permitted to interconnect

bins.name: Preserve State Retail Authority
bins.description: Entergy argues that FERC's transmission jurisdiction does not extend to retail service rates, terms, or unbundling. Any final rule must be explicit that states retain authority over retail service agreements and that the Commission is not requiring retail unbundling in states that have not adopted it.

bins.name: Raise Threshold to 100 MW
bins.description: Entergy opposes the proposed 20 MW threshold as too low. A 20 MW threshold would quadruple the number of qualifying loads in Entergy territories, create administrative overload, and invite speculative projects. Entergy recommends at least 100 MW, consistent with thresholds used by the White House EO, Georgia PSC, and other state programs.

bins.name: No Mandatory Federal Queue
bins.description: Entergy opposes any uniform federal interconnection queue for loads, arguing it would be inefficient and litigation-prone. Instead, utilities should be allowed to propose region-specific study processes through FPA Section 205 filings.

bins.name: Deter Speculative Loads
bins.description: Entergy agrees that steps to deter speculative loads from clogging interconnection queues are reasonable and should be part of any final rule.

bins.name: Full Study for Hybrid Loads
bins.description: Entergy opposes studying hybrid facilities on a net injection or withdrawal basis, warning that net-basis studies miss large actual load flows, underidentify needed upgrades, and create reliability risks from variable generator output and withdrawal patterns.

bins.name: Generation Adequacy Before Interconnection
bins.description: Entergy argues that large loads should not interconnect without verifying adequate generation capacity. Resource adequacy review is a state retail-jurisdictional matter that the Commission must not displace.

bins.name: Full Cost Responsibility for New Loads
bins.description: Entergy insists that large loads must pay the full cost of transmission upgrades they cause, with states retaining jurisdiction over retail cost allocation questions.

bins.name: Joint Load-Generation Study
bins.description: Entergy supports the principle that load and generation additions should be studied together for efficiency, but opposes applying a uniform federal study process and requires that hybrid loads be studied on a gross rather than net basis to capture actual reliability impacts.

bins.name: New Loads Bear Full Upgrade Costs
bins.description: Entergy supports the principle that large loads pay their full share of upgrade costs but stresses that states retain jurisdiction over how those costs are allocated in retail rate proceedings.

bins.name: Reliability Risk from Net-Basis Study
bins.description: Entergy opposes the proposed net-basis treatment for co-located hybrid facilities, arguing it is too simplistic and will leave real injection and withdrawal risks unidentified, threatening grid reliability.

bins.name: Regional Flexibility Over Uniformity
bins.description: Entergy strongly supports regional flexibility and opposes any uniform national rule that does not account for differences among states and RTOs. MISO South's existing processes work well and should not be disrupted by blanket federal requirements.

bins.name: MISO South Processes Are Adequate
bins.description: Entergy operates in MISO South under a vertically integrated, retail cost-of-service model and reports that existing MISO and state processes are working to connect large loads. Federal rules must not override these regional frameworks.
